Representative_Docs: ["use multi gpu(mirrored strategy) training with xla and amp(mixed precision) hi i've encountered some issues while trying to perform multi-gpu training with xla (accelerated linear algebra), and amp (automatic mixed precision). i'm reaching out to understand if it's possible to use multi-gpu training with xla and amp together. if so, i'd like guidance on which versions of tensorflow and keras should i use or how to modify my code to make this work. background: in earlier versions of tensorflow (prior to 2.11), we were able to successfully train models using multiple gpus with both xla and amp enabled. however, with versions beyond tensorflow 2.11 versions, i've not been able to run training with multi gpu xla amp. issues encountered with different versions: i use tf-keras 2.15 for all these tests, 1. tensorflow 2.17.1 2.16.2 and keras 3.8.0: error message runtimeerror: exception encountered when calling cond.call() mergecall called while defining a new graph or a tf.function. this can often happen if the function fn passed to strategy.run() contains a nested .function, and the nested .function contains a synchronization point, such as aggregating gradients (e.g, optimizer.applygradients), or if the function fn uses a control flow statement which contains a synchronization point in the body. such behaviors are not yet supported. instead, please avoid nested tf.functions or control flow statements that may potentially cross a synchronization boundary, for example, wrap the fn passed to strategy.run or the entire strategy.run inside a tf.function or move the control flow out of fn. if you are subclassing a tf.keras.model, please avoid decorating overridden methods teststep and trainstep in tf.function 2. tensorflow 2.17.1 2.16.2 and keras 3.5.0: issue: 3. tensorflow 2.17.1 2.16.2 and keras 3.0.5 error message: 4. tensorflow 2.18.0 also gives similar error with keras versions 3.0,3.5 and 3.6 5. using training gets stuck after some time similarly as with keras 3. i have tried this with various tensorflow versions but got same training stuck. code snippet: here's a simplified version of the code i'm using. this example is adapted from the keras documentation on distributed training ( can someone suggest what changes i should make in code or which version of keras and tensorflow to use to make training with multi gpu xla amp work? or is it not possible train using multi gpu xla amp?", "here is the code with slight modification that works with import keras from keras.models import sequential from keras.layers import lstm, dense, input, inputlayer xtrain keras.random.normal((50,20,3)) ytrain keras.random.normal((50, 1)) model sequential() model.add(inputlayer(batchinputshape xtrain.shape)) model.add(lstm(units 4, stateful true)) model.add(dense(1)) model.compile(loss 'meansquarederror', optimizer 'adam') model.fit(xtrain, ytrain, epochs 100, batchsize 1,) hello! could you create a similar example with simplernn layer using functional api instead? i am having issues reproducing the logic using tensorflow 2.18 and keras 3.8. and functional api, when training the model. so replace lstm with rnn with stateful true and keep the dense layer. however, the official documentation states the following: else for functional model with 1 or more input layers: to all the first layers in your model. this is the expected shape of your inputs including the batch size. it should be a tuple of integers, e.g. . - specify when calling . to reset the states of your model, call on either a specific layer, or on your entire model. not sure how .reset_states() is to be combined with stateful true and whether it makes sense? thank you for your time.", "issue error you're encountering stems from an issue with using masking in the embedding layer for pos tagging model the error you're encountering stems from an issue with using masking in the embedding layer (maskzero true) and passing that through subsequent layers like lstm and timedistributed. here's how you can address the issue: key points to fix: mask propagation: ensure that layers following the embedding layer correctly handle the mask. the lstm layer natively supports masking, but there may be issues when passing the mask to timedistributed. loss function: the sparsecategoricalcrossentropy expects integer labels, and masking should be handled properly in the output to avoid issues. input shapes: check the compatibility of shapes being passed through the layers. eager execution: if you're using tensorflow 2.x, ensure eager execution is enabled (which is the default mode in tf 2.x). here's the corrected and modified version of your code: python copy edit import tensorflow as tf from tensorflow import keras define the model architecture model keras.sequential( keras.input(shape (200,)), input shape matches the padded sequence length keras.layers.embedding(inputdim vocablen, outputdim 50, weights embeddingmatrix , maskzero true), enable masking for padding tokens keras.layers.bidirectional(keras.layers.lstm(units 100, returnsequences true)), lstm supports masking keras.layers.bidirectional(keras.layers.lstm(units 100, returnsequences true)), keras.layers.timedistributed(keras.layers.dense(units tagslen, activation softmax )) timedistributed for sequence output ) compile the model model.compile( optimizer adam , loss sparsecategoricalcrossentropy , suitable for integer labels metrics accuracy ) display model summary model.summary() training model.fit(xtrain, ytrain, epochs 10) changes and fixes: masking compatibility: layers like lstm and bidirectional handle the mask propagated from the embedding layer. there's no need for manual intervention. output layer: the timedistributed layer is designed to handle the sequence output of the lstm layer. loss function: ensure ytrain is formatted correctly for sparsecategoricalcrossentropy, with integer labels and no one-hot encoding. debugging: if the issue persists, wrap the model.fit call in tf.function: python copy edit .function def train(): model.fit(xtrain, ytrain, epochs 10) train() additional tips: masking with timedistributed: if the problem persists in the timedistributed layer, consider explicitly handling the mask: python copy edit keras.layers.timedistributed(keras.layers.dense(units tagslen, activation softmax ), inputshape (none, 200, 100)) debugging with eager execution: ensure eager execution is enabled by adding: python copy edit tf.config.runfunctionseagerly(true) data validation: confirm that xtrain and ytrain are correctly padded to the same sequence length (200) and formatted as numpy arrays."]

Name: 0_keras_model_issue_shape
Representative_Docs: ['silently returns zero-dimension output in symbolic mode instead of raising an error for invalid parameters. related keras version keras 3.13.1, keras 3.10.0 the layer shows inconsistent behavior between eager and symbolic execution. when given parameters that are invalid for the input shape (e.g., a that makes the effective kernel larger than the input), it correctly raises an error in eager mode. however, in symbolic mode, it fails silently and returns a with a zero-dimension shape (e.g., ). this can mask configuration errors during model construction, leading to downstream issues that are difficult to debug. the symbolic path should perform the same validation and fail fast. this issue is observed across different backends (tensorflow 2.20.0, pytorch 2.10.0 cu128). code to reproduce actual behavior eager execution fails with a descriptive error, but symbolic execution proceeds and produces a tensor with a zero dimension. (note: the exact error message varies by backend, but the eager failure is consistent.) expected behavior symbolic execution should also raise an error during graph construction, mirroring the eager behavior. this would provide immediate feedback that the layer configuration is invalid.', 'layer with produces incorrect symbolic output shape for nested input tensors system information keras version: 3.13.2 backend: tensorflow 2.20.0 os: linux code to reproduce expected behavior the symbolic output shape should be consistent with the eager execution output shape. given an input tensor with a shape of , the layer with should produce an output tensor with a shape of . for the provided example, the symbolic output shape should be . actual behavior the symbolic execution incorrectly infers the output shape, resulting in .', "deserializing error when loading models from '.keras' files in keras 3, issue with dense layers i am using google colab with the tensorflow v2.17 and keras v 3.4.1 libraries. i need to save and load my models, but i haven't been able to make the '.keras' file format load correctly. here is the line for saving the model: here is the line for loading the model: this is my error: this is the model that i trained: this model was just used for testing the bug. i have used as an alternative for loading the model, but i received the same error. interestingly, when i run the code for the first time, this is included in the error output. when the same code is run again, the line is no longer included: i have tested the code on the latest keras v3.5, and have gotten similiar results: i have tested the bug again by saving and loading the model into separate weights and json files: the error is at least slightly different: ultimately it would be a lot better to find out that i've been doing something wrong and i can fix this problem myself. i've been hung up on this for awhile, and i have a thesis to write."]

Representative_Docs: ["truncates multiple loss inputs environment issue upon first call of , is triggered, which fails if is e.g. a tuple and the loss function expects , as only the first element gets passed to the loss function. details let's say i have a loss function i.e. the ground truth consists of two tensors (i can't stack them, their size is incompatible) and the prediction from my model is a single tensor. let's also say i have a model that takes two inputs and and produces one output . due to the nature of my problem, the input is automatically the ground truth, i.e. . so i would like to execute where the last call causes an exception in , because only gets passed as to . i have pinpointed the problem to the call to here ( and the subsequent iteration here ( the problem seems to be that the zip iteration expects and to be iterables of the same length as (which in my case is just 1). now wraps the single tensor into a single element list, which is correct. however, converts from a 2 element tuple to a 2 element list, where it should be a nested list with a single 2-element list . consequently the zip iteration takes only from in its single iteration (since all other iterables are just length one) and passes it as argument to . i imagine this behavior comes from the fact that in cases where one has multiple losses (which take single tensors as and ), the correct way of calling is to pass sequences for and to , one for each loss function. isn't there a way to reconcile both cases? i.e. sequence inputs to single loss functions, but still supporting multiple loss functions? all the information is there, i.e. how many loss functions and what are their signatures. for starters, it is not checked if all elements in the iteration are of same length... reproducible example here is a reproducible minimal example which produces the following error many thanks for all the great effort on keras, i greatly appreciate all the awesome features!", "custom loss function only receives one first output from model declaring multiple outputs current behavior declare a model with 2 named outputs, one for the bounding box and the second for the label classification. implicit declaration of loss functions in the compile configuration works ok. explicit declaration of a customloss function in the compile configuration calls the customloss function, but it only receives the first output (bbox), not the expected two (bbox labels) standalone code to reproduce the issue model as (numclasses 6 for ex): bbox layers.dense(4, name bbox )(features) classificationoutput layers.dense(numclasses, name classification , activation softmax )(features) model keras.model(inputs inputs, outputs bbox, classificationoutput , name 'vitobjectdetectorwithclass') dictionary for bounding box loss bboxlossdict mseloss : tf.keras.losses.meansquarederror(), mean squared error (for bounding box regression) maeloss : tf.keras.losses.meanabsoluteerror() mean absolute error (alternative for bounding boxes) dictionary for classification loss classlossdict sparsecategoricalcrossentropy : tf.keras.losses.sparsecategoricalcrossentropy(fromlogits false), cross-entropy loss for multi-class classification categoricalcrossentropy : tf.keras.losses.categoricalcrossentropy(fromlogits false) another option for multi-class classification if using one-hot encoded labels implicit declaration of configuration, inclusive the losses that works ok: model.compile( optimizer 'adam', or any optimizer loss bbox : bboxlossdict mseloss , classification : classlossdict sparsecategoricalcrossentropy , lossweights bbox : 1.0, classification : 1.5 , metrics bbox : mse , mae , classification : accuracy ) training: targets bbox : bboxtarget, shape, for ex: (640,4) classification : classtarget shape for ex: (640,) model.fit(xtrain, targets, epochs 10, batchsize 32) that training works correctly. declaring explicitly a customloss function: def customloss(ytrue, ypred): bboxtrue ytrue 0 bounding boxes ground truth classtrue ytrue 1 class labels ground truth bboxpred ypred 0 bounding box predictions classpred ypred 1 class predictions ...... etc and declaring the configuration as: model.compile(optimizer 'adam', loss customloss, metrics accuracy ) the customloss function only receives the bbox data (ypred.shape (32,4)) but not the label classification. it should be something like: y_pred.shape (32,4),(32,)", "thanks for the comment! i had thought about such a solution. unfortunately, it involves calculating all losses twice. some of my losses are a bit costly. for neural vocoders it may involve calculate the mel spectrogram from the synthesized signal. but i think this can be made better when the combined loss internally calculates the loss and as well the metrics and that allows accessing the metrics such that they can be accessed in the metrics property. the loss would be like this and i'd need to instantiate the loss and store it somewhere and then overwrite the metrics property of the model such that it gathers the metrics from the loss. what is unfortunate in that case is that i will not be able to use the official compiledmetrics container, which - as far as i see - would require me to establish the handling of training in distributed environments (multiple gpus). i might fix this by means of not using metrics.mean but a derived class that overwrites the update_state method such that it takes an additional argument ignored so that Representative_Docs: ["hi roman, absolutely, if you want to work on an openvino backend we'd be happy to support you. we don't really bandwidth to actively develop new backends (e.g. our mlx backend is not making progress due to lack of resources) but we're able to answer your questions and review any prs you submit. adding openvino as a new backend to support inference does openvino not support gradients? or is it otherwise not a good fit for training?", "so the plan is to support keras api for inference in case openvino backend. don't you mind us to add openvino due to this limitation ? we already have a backend that is inference-only, the numpy backend. this is not a big issue. i see that we need to add openvino directory into backends with implemented helpers for keras op decompositions using openvino opset. anything else can you recommend to take a look? or other useful developer documentation? since you're only targeting inference, there's a lot you won't need to implement -- no trainer class, no optimizers. i suggest you look the most closely at what is implemented in , since that's the other inference-only backend. if something is confusing, you can look at how it's implemented in other backends -- you have 3 other reference implementations for each function. i think the main point of difficulty might be static shape inference ( ), depending on how openvino works. now we plan to estimate required work, implement protype to infer some basic network and get back. great -- don't hesitate to reach out with questions!", "openvino backend: gfis for further development a great opportunity to contribute to two popular ai projects with just one pr:: keras 3 ( and openvino ( keras 3 enables seamless switching between supported backends pytorch, tensorflow, and jax for both training and inference of traditional models and llms genai pipelines. since keras 3.8.0, we've introduced a preview version of the openvino backend (for inference only), allowing developers to leverage openvino for model predictions directly within keras 3 workflows. activating the openvino backend requires just one line of code to run inference on keras 3-trained models. here s an example for a bert model from keras hub: currently, the openvino backend lacks support for some operations. our goal is to resolve this gap and to optimize it for inference on intel devices including cpus, integrated gpus, discrete gpus, and npus by supporting as many models as possible while delivering optimal performance. we aim to make the openvino backend the no. 1 choice for model inference within the keras 3 workflow . we warmly welcome you to participate in further development of the openvino backend. here is a list of good-first-issues (it will be periodically updated with new ones): - - - - - - - - - already done issues - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -"]

Representative_Docs: ["you're right, gradient sum reduction wasn't enabled by default with a . this was a regression. we have switched back the default behavior to sum-reduction. please confirm that the fix at head is working for your use case. to be clear, you do not need the argument, you only need the default behavior to be sum-reduction? we have no current support for actual multi-worker pss at this time. custom training loop mixed precision training is this a custom ? support mixed precision takes just one line: make sure to call on your loss after computing it. everything else is built-in. xla in a custom you don't need to do anything specific to support xla compilation or , it's all built-in. then for best performance, i cast the gradient to fp16 -- all reduce -- cast back to fp32 -- unscale lossscale -- apply gradient (with skipgradientsaggregation true args). so i need finegrain control of the gradient to satisfy my use case if what you need is to cast gradients to float16 before reduction, then what i suggest is to write an optimizer subclass that does this. here's the part of the code that's relevant: just cast the grads before and cast them back after. no need for a custom training loop here. thanks, i will have a try. but i found that the unscale gradient apply very early at the applygradient api, which produce unscale fp32 gradient, if subclass the opt (follow your suggestion), the unscale fp32 gradient will cast to fp16, then which may lead to produce underflow gradient ... (just guess)", "feature request add neat (nash-equilibrium adaptive training) optimizer to keras.optimizers summary this issue proposes adding neat (nash-equilibrium adaptive training) as a first-class optimizer in . neat is a conflict-aware momentum optimizer that introduces a lightweight nash-inspired correction term to suppress gradient components that directly conflict with the optimizer's own momentum history. the result is a smoother, more stable update rule that degrades gracefully to standard sgd-with-momentum when no conflict is detected. i am the author of the reference implementation at and am prepared to deliver a complete, review-ready pr against following all contribution guidelines. --- motivation every optimizer in today sgd, adam, adamw, adagrad, rmsprop, nadam, ftrl, adamax shares the same fundamental assumption: the gradient at step is always the correct direction to move. none of them model directional conflict between the current gradient and the optimizer's own accumulated signal. in practice, conflict is common: - noisy or oscillatory loss surfaces (e.g. small-batch training, transformers with warm-up) - multi-task multi-domain fine-tuning where different data sources pull in opposing directions - recurrent and sequence models where gradients change direction across time steps - adversarial or contrastive objectives where the loss landscape is intentionally non-convex when conflict occurs, a standard optimizer blindly accumulates the conflicting gradient into momentum, causing oscillation and slower convergence. neat detects conflict via the cosine similarity between and the opponent proxy , and removes the conflicting projection before accumulating momentum. this is directly analogous to how pcgrad (yu et al., neurips 2020) and cagrad (liu et al., neurips 2021) handle multi-task gradient conflict but neat does it within a single optimizer pass with no per-task gradient storage, making it usable via with zero changes to the training loop. --- algorithm for parameter tensor , gradient , opponent proxy (default: previous momentum ), correction strength , and momentum decay : key properties: - when (gradient and opponent are aligned or orthogonal), and the update is identical to sgd with momentum. no regression on tasks where conflict is absent. - the correction magnitude is bounded: by construction, so it cannot destabilize training. - the opponent proxy is configurable: (default), , or . - a warmup gate ( ) and a conflict threshold gate ( ) allow safe deployment when early gradients are noisy. --- proposed api usage (identical to any other keras optimizer): the optimizer is fully serializable via and compatible with all three keras backends (tensorflow, jax, pytorch). --- relationship to existing optimizers property sgd adam neat --- --- --- --- momentum yes ema (1st moment) yes adaptive lr per param no yes (2nd moment) no (base version) conflict detection no no yes decoupled weight decay no adamw variant yes (built-in) reduces to sgd when no conflict no yes extra memory per param 0 1 tensor 1 3 tensors (m, prevg, gema) neat occupies a distinct position: it is not a second-moment method and does not adapt the learning rate per parameter. instead it modifies which direction the gradient pushes, making it orthogonal to and composable with adaptive methods. --- benchmarks the reference repository includes a reproducible keras mlp benchmark on the digits dataset (8x8 images, 10 classes, 3 seeds each): optimizer mean test accuracy (20 epochs) --- --- sgd (momentum 0.9) 97.04 adam 96.85 adamw 96.85 neat (alpha 0.25, projection) 94.72 neat trails adam sgd on this smooth, low-noise benchmark. this is expected and honest: the conflict correction has a mean ratio of only on this task (the gradients are largely aligned), so neat is effectively running as sgd with a small overhead. the optimizer's advantage surfaces on tasks with genuine gradient conflict multi-task objectives, noisy fine-tuning, and oscillatory landscapes which are precisely the use cases not well-served by the current optimizer suite. diagnostic tooling ( ) is included in the reference implementation to measure the live conflict ratio during training, allowing users to verify whether their task benefits from the correction. --- implementation plan if this proposal is accepted, i will deliver: 1. full subclass following the exact same structure as , , and with: - creating , , and slot variables - implementing the update rule above, using only (backend-agnostic) - for full serialization - a complete numpy-style docstring with args, math description, and usage examples - input validation with clear error messages 2. add to the exports 3. public api export 4. unit tests covering: - reduces to sgd with momentum (numerical equality) - correction is bounded by - warmup gate: zero correction for first steps - conflict threshold gate: correction suppressed below threshold - round-trip serialization - slot variable shapes match variable shapes - convergence on quadratic loss (x² 0) within 100 steps - all three modes run without error - weight decay applies parameter shrinkage correctly 5. documentation docstring follows the same format as and will be auto-included in the api reference. all code will use only (no backend-specific calls), will pass and , and will be tested against the full keras ci matrix. --- reference - reference implementation: (apache-2.0) - math specification: - related work: pcgrad (yu et al., neurips 2020), cagrad (liu et al., neurips 2021), conflict-averse gradient descent --- i would appreciate any feedback on the proposed api surface before submitting the pr. happy to adjust the parameter names, defaults, or scope based on maintainer guidance."]

Name: 9_extraction_paths_import_files
Representative_Docs: ["attributeerror in zip extraction: 'zipinfo' object has no attribute 'name' (introduced in 21760) after pr 21760 (which added safe archive extraction logic), calling extractopenarchive with a zip archive immediately raises anattributeerror: attributeerror: 'zipinfo' object has no attribute 'name' this breaks zip extraction for model loading, weights, and datasets. the error comes from using finfo.name in filtersafezipinfos, while zipinfo only has finfo.filename. expected behavior 1. safe files should extract normally 2.unsafe paths should be skipped with a warning 3.no attributeerror during zip extraction steps to reproduce import io import zipfile from keras.src.utils import fileutils zipbuffer io.bytesio() with zipfile.zipfile(zipbuffer, w ) as zf: zf.writestr( test.txt , sample content ) zipbuffer.seek(0) with zipfile.zipfile(zipbuffer, r ) as archive: fileutils.extractopenarchive(archive, path . extractedtest ) root cause: typo in keras src utils fileutils.py filtersafezipinfos uses finfo.name instead of finfo.filename. fixed code: def filtersafezipinfos(members): basedir resolvepath( . ) for finfo in members: validpath false fix: replace .name with .filename for zipinfo if ispathindir(finfo.filename, basedir): validpath true yield finfo if not valid_path: warnings.warn( skipping invalid path during archive extraction: f ' finfo.filename '. , stacklevel 2, )", 'doc security consideration: callback file paths accept absolute and traversal paths description while working with keras callbacks (modelcheckpoint, csvlogger, and tensorboard), i observed that file paths provided by the user are used directly without normalization or restriction. for example: absolute paths (e.g., tmp model.keras) are written as-is relative paths with traversal components (e.g., .. .. etc passwd.keras) are also accepted poc observed result expected behavior this behavior is expected in trusted environments. however, it would be helpful if: the documentation clearly states that callback file paths are used without validation guidance is provided for safely handling paths when input may be untrusted actual behavior keras callbacks use the provided file paths directly without any normalization or restriction. absolute paths are accepted and written to directly relative paths with traversal components ( ) are resolved by the operating system and may write files outside the current working directory no warnings or validation are performed on the provided paths as a result, files are created at the specified locations if the process has sufficient permissions. suggested improvement document clearly that callbacks accept user provided paths without restriction. consider offering an optional safe mode or path validation utility for applications that may consume untrusted input. at minimum, warn developers in the docs that paths should be sanitized at the application level.', 'extractarchive leaves incomplete inconsistent directory state when a target file is os-locked summary leaves behind a partially extracted directory when an os-level file lock causes to fail. keras does not perform cleanup because is not included in the exception list. this breaks atomic extraction and can cause corrupted caches, incomplete model directories, and inconsistent behavior especially on windows or nfs filesystems where file locking is common. --- reproduction script observed result the directory remains in an inconsistent state ( locked partial extraction of ). --- expected behavior if extraction fails for any reason, keras should: - remove the extraction directory entirely or - guarantee fully atomic extraction (temp rename) partial extraction should not remain. --- root cause analysis the cleanup logic catches only: but file locking raises: since is not included, the cleanup block is skipped and the directory persists. relevant code (in ): --- suggested fix option a (minimal patch) add : option b (recommended) perform extraction into a temporary directory and atomically rename upon success. --- environment - os: windows 11 (reliable reproduction) - python: 3.13.5 - keras: 3.14.0 --- conclusion this is a correctness robustness bug: extraction failures caused by os-level file locks leave incomplete directories because cleanup is not triggered. a small change to the exception handling or atomic extraction strategy resolves the issue.']

Name: 15_bar_progress bar_progress_metric
Representative_Docs: ["values in 's progress bar do not match final output for certain metrics (e.g. auc) comuted in , the values shown in the progress bar are misleading, and the final state in the progress bar does not reflect the actual metric value. here's a code sample that reproduces the issue, tested in using torch backend (but the behavior is the same across backends). the output of this code is below. note the specific values are rng dependent, but the point still stands. which shows that the final auc computed using both and manually, but the auc value shown in the progress bar is different. in some cases the differences are quite large.", 'evaluate() shows incorrect metric values in progress bar vs returned results when using model.evaluate(), the metric values displayed in the progress bar differ from the values returned by the method. there appears to be double averaging happening - the batch values are already averaged, but the progress bar shows an additional average of these averages. code to reproduce: code to reproduce: output: expected behavior: the metric values shown in the progress bar should match the final returned results (or at least be clearly documented if this difference is intentional). issue: progress bar shows loss: 17.8182, mae: 3.4545 returned values show loss: 38.5, mae: 5.5 the correct values should be the returned ones (38.5 and 5.5 respectively), as these match manual calculations the progress bar seems to be averaging already-averaged batch values environment: tensorflow 2.19 additional notes: this discrepancy can be confusing for users who rely on the progress bar metrics during evaluation.', "that metric in the progress bar is a training metric, computed on the training data. there are n different reasons a training metric might turn out different from the metric you see in a call to (e.g. the state of the model changes at each step, and training behavior might be different from inference behavior). afaik, just takes one dataset so it doesn't distinguish between train test val. i understand the progress bar i see during reflects the changing state of the model, however the model weights should be frozen during , and the model should be in inference mode. so based on what you said i don't see why the metrics in 's progress bar shouldn't converge to the final metric. actually the metric values shown in the progress bar are mean values, i.e. mean auc of 32 steps. if this is true, and the progress bar shows a running average of the metric computed over batches, then it is understandable the answers are different. however i still think it's misleading and a better choice would be to show the result of the metric after incorporating each batch. (i.e. the progress bar should show )"]

Name: 19_seed_random_deterministic_seeds
Representative_Docs: ['potential error in documentation of keras.random.uniform. in the documentation of keras.ranom.uniform i find seed: a python integer or instance of . used to make the behavior of the initializer deterministic. note that an initializer seeded with an integer or none (unseeded) will produce the same random values across multiple calls. to get different random values across multiple calls, use as seed an instance of . for me this implies that which however is not the case. see the colab , seed none) produces a sequence of random numbers. on the other hand really creates the same number for all calls in keras but not in tensorflow, which is what i was expecting from the documentation. am i misunderstanding this or is the documentation about wrong?', 'keras.utils.setrandomseed does not set seeds os: windows 11 python 3.11.0 64 bit keras 3.0.5 ---- keras.utils.setrandomseed() does not set the reproducibility for (at least) numpy operations. import numpy as np import keras seed 0 keras.utils.setrandomseed(seed seed) randomnumbergenerator np.random.defaultrng() gamma randomnumbergenerator.gamma(shape 2, size (100, 1)) multiple runs will produce different random values. with randomnumbergenerator np.random.defaultrng(seed seed) multiple runs will produce same random values.', 'feature add deterministic training mode in keras description currently, achieving reproducible training results in keras is non-trivial, even when explicitly setting random seeds. in practice, identical seeds, data, and model configurations can still produce significantly different outputs. there is no unified, high-level way in keras to enforce deterministic behavior across: - layer initialization - optimizer updates - backend operations --- rationale reproducibility is essential for: - scientific experiments - debugging workflows - model comparison and validation - production reliability at present, users must manually configure backend-specific settings, and deterministic behavior is not consistently guaranteed. --- proposed feature introduce a simple, high-level api: this would: - enforce deterministic backend behavior where possible - configure random seeds consistently across components - disable or warn about nondeterministic operations - provide a unified and user-friendly reproducibility mode --- proof of concept the following example demonstrates non-deterministic behavior despite fixed seeds: --- observed behavior --- expected behavior with deterministic mode enabled: expected output: --- impact - improves reproducibility across training runs - simplifies debugging and experimentation - aligns with expectations in research and production environments - reduces hidden nondeterministic behavior --- additional notes tensorflow provides partial support for deterministic execution, but this is not exposed through a simple, unified keras api. a keras-level abstraction would significantly improve usability and reliability.']

Name: 22_deserialization_custom_objects_serialization
Representative_Docs: ['bug deserializekerasobject accepts callable objects in config without validation description accepts callable objects ( functions or lambdas) in configuration fields without validation. these callables are stored and later invoked during layer execution (e.g., during ), which can lead to unexpected runtime behavior. reproduction script build ) no error is raised during deserialization expected behavior configuration should be restricted to serializable and validated types callable objects should be rejected or explicitly handled deserialization should not allow arbitrary callables in config notes keras emits a warning when serializing lambda functions, but during deserialization callable objects are still accepted and executed. this may lead to unexpected or unsafe behavior if configuration input is not fully trusted.', 'hi , from the error log it seems you have a custom loss function which is not serialized. custom loss functions or classes have to be serialized explicitly by registering the class function with decorator. for custom classes you might need additional work to implement and or methods explicitly. please refer to the attached guide ( for more details on custom objects.', "thank you for the clarification. on tue, mar 19, 2024 at 12:17 pm surya . wrote: hi from the error log it seems you have a custom loss function 'mse' which is not serialized. custom loss functions or classes have to be serialized explicitly by registering the class function with .saving.registerkerasserializable() decorator. for custom classes you might need additional work to implement getconfig and or fromconfig methods explicitly. please refer to the attached guide custom objects. reply to this email directly, view it on github or unsubscribe . you are receiving this because you were mentioned.message id: ."]

Name: 23_crop_center_image_resize
Representative_Docs: ["one more thing to add, sometimes you may need to resize the validation instead of center crop. although center crop is more common it may still be beneficial to allow resizing of validation images maybe through an argument that can accept either: 'resize' or 'centercrop' with a default of centercrop what do you think?", 'randomcrop does nothing at validation inference the randomcrop layer does absolutely nothing during validation. this is due to the if condition placed here: this looks pretty odd as the validation split will have different shape from the training data. the logic seemed to be it should do center crop for as it is calculated here: example of training with 512x512 images but doing random crop to 256x512 confirms this as it raises this error as soon as the epoch finishes. please confirm if this is an intended behaviour that you must place a center crop layer with the same crop size just after it to maintain the pipeline?', "i'm somewhat inclined to leave the behavior as is, as i don't see an obvious always right solution for what to do at inference time. you might want to... 1. run inference at the full image size despite training at a smaller size, for models that don't have a fixed input size requirement (most convnets). e.g. resnet models are frequently tested with images at a slightly larger scale ( than training. 2. resize the model to the target size without cropping for inference (with any of the arguments to the resizing layer). 3. center crop the image. i'd actually suspect that most users would probably want here, since throwing away image during training can make sense to augment and stretch your data, but will almost always result in loss of quality at inference time vs a resizing op that does not throw out portions of the image. so it's probably better to ask users to be explicit here. either only apply the during training, and apply or during inference. or if you really want to use the same preprocessing pipeline at training and inference, simply chain the into a or layer (where the latter will be a no-op during training, since images will already be the target size, and only specifies the inference behavior). does that make sense ? if the center cropping during inference was going to be the right thing for 95 of users i'd be onboard with your proposal, but i don't think that's the case here. most users should resize at inference. i think it would be very reasonable to add a flag to all the random layers (so they still do there thing at inference time). but a random crop should be a random crop always if it's appled. and use center crop if you want a center crop."]

Name: 24_variance_mean_gh_normalization
Representative_Docs: ["normalization layer fails to broadcast scalar mean and variance normalization layer errors out for scalar mean and variance arguments. the documentation states that these arguments will be broadcast to the necessary size and the examples include scalars, but instead the source code seems to only reshape them (see here ( i'm using python 3.12.1 and keras 3.6.0.", 'keras.layers.normalization crashes with multi-dim mean and variance crash with valid multi-dim mean and variance please use this example to replicate the problem: error message: i believe we can reshape the tensor of 2, 4 to tensor of shape 1,2,1,4 given 2x4 1x2x1x4. in keras 3.7.0, keras.layers.normalization worked well with these inputs. however, from keras 3.8.0, this error happens. i found that this may be a regression introduced in gh-20626. i think we can just seperate two cases, one for float, i.e., the case of gh-20626 where we will use and one for matrices where we use same as previous implementation. if this approach make senses to you, i can make a pr.', 'hi and maintainers thanks for the quick investigation and for pointing to gh-20626. i m happy to take this on and open a pr. summary of proposed fix root cause the change introduced in gh-20626 uses tf.broadcastto when preparing mean variance for the normalization layer. this fails in cases where mean variance are provided as lower-rank numeric arrays intended to be reshaped (not strictly broadcasted) into the specified axes. in these cases, tf.broadcastto raises an invalidargumenterror when it cannot match the target shape, even though an equivalent reshape would be valid. this worked correctly prior to 3.8. proposal restore the previous behavior for the matrix-like stats case, while preserving the improvements from gh-20626, by distinguishing between two scenarios: reshape case (restore pre-3.8 behavior) when mean variance are provided as multi-dimensional arrays whose total number of elements matches the product of the target axis sizes, and whose rank matches len(axis), treat them as values laid out to fill those axes and reshape them into the expanded axes shape. broadcast case (keep gh-20626 behavior) for scalars, per-axis 1-d values, or cases that genuinely require broadcasting, continue to use tf.broadcastto as introduced in gh-20626. this keeps the new behavior for stateless broadcastable cases while reintroducing reshape semantics for matrix-shaped statistics that previously worked. patch outline (high level) in the normalization layer code path that prepares mean variance: convert stats to tensors. compute the target broadcast shape from the input shape and axis. check whether: stat.ndim len(axis), and numelements(stat) productoftargetaxessizes if so, reshape the stats into the expanded shape (1s in non-axis dims). otherwise, fall back to tf.broadcastto as in gh-20626. add comments explaining the two branches and reference gh-20626. example branching logic (pseudo-code) stat tf.converttotensor(stat) inputshape tf.shape(inputs) dynamic rank tf.rank(inputs) build target broadcast shape: 1, ..., sizeonaxisi, ..., 1 targetshape 1 rank for i, ax in enumerate(axis): targetshape ax inputshape ax statnumel tf.size(stat) axesproduct tf.reduceprod( targetshape ax for ax in axis ) reshape vs broadcast if (tf.shape(stat).ndims len(axis)) and tf.equal(statnumel, axesproduct): reshapeshape 1 rank for i, ax in enumerate(axis): reshapeshape ax tf.shape(stat) i stat tf.reshape(stat, reshapeshape) else: stat tf.broadcastto(stat, target_shape) tests to add regression test reproducing the reported issue: input shape (none, 2, 3, 4) axis (1, 3) mean variance shape (2, 4) ensure forward pass succeeds and output shape matches input. additional coverage: scalar mean variance 1-d per-axis stats (broadcast path) dynamic input shape case (tf.shape(inputs)) reference gh-20626 in test comments. compatibility considerations restores pre-3.8 behavior for matrix-shaped statistics. preserves gh-20626 improvements for broadcast-only cases. should be backward-compatible and safe for dynamic shapes. if maintainers prefer a different heuristic (e.g., stricter shape checks or limiting reshape to non-scalar stats), i m happy to adapt. next steps i can draft and open a pr with: the fix unit tests a short regression note referencing gh-20626 please let me know if you prefer a specific branch, style, or implementation detail. thanks!']

Name: 25_model_callback_backupandrestore_training
Representative_Docs: ['the backup callback only saves no optimizer state information are stored. consider this case: you train a model with epoch 50. you use warmup learninge rate and various scheduler setup. you also use backup callback. now, your training interrupted at epoch 27. now, you restarted the environment - then import modules, build model and compile again. can the previous backup resume training from epoch 27 with last check optimizer state? if you say, with this backup, internally the keras will compute last check optimizer state and resume training, can we verify this.', "how would we ensure the optimizer state load properly? isn't .h5 only store model param? what about .keras format. does it hold only model pram or optimizer param both? what about keras.callbacks.backupandrestore ( - see this ( to ensure the ability to recover from an interrupted training run at any time (fault tolerance), you should use a . import numpy as np import keras class interruptingcallback(keras.callbacks.callback): a callback to intentionally introduce interruption to training. def onepochend(self, epoch, log none): if epoch 15: raise runtimeerror('interruption') model keras.sequential( keras.layers.dense(10) ) optimizer keras.optimizers.sgd() model.compile(optimizer, loss mse ) x np.random.random((24, 10)) y np.random.random((24,)) backupcallback keras.callbacks.experimental.backupandrestore( backupdir ' tmp backup') try: model.fit(x, y, epochs 20, stepsperepoch 5, callbacks backupcallback, interruptingcallback() ) except runtimeerror: print('handling interruption') this continues at the epoch where it left off. model.fit(x, y, epochs 20, stepsperepoch 5, callbacks backupcallback )", "yes - in the case computer crash kernel restart need to build model again after interruption because model configuration(weights,optimizer..) are lost. so for that case need to rebuild the model. if model architecture and nodel configuration doesn't change then no need to reinstantiated after interruption i see a conflict of my understanding here. once training proceeds as per the model data at 'backup_dir'. unless this directory lost it is expected to restore the model state and resume training again right? for even kernel restart or crash this is expected to save model's latest state and restore it again once training resume back."]
